learning_outcomes.description: Analyse major literary texts, writers, and movements from approximately 1850 to 1960.
Interpret literary works in relation to relevant historical, cultural, political, and intellectual contexts
Evaluate continuities and changes between Victorian and modern literature through comparative analysis.
Develop and communicate evidence-based arguments through close reading, research, and critical engagement with literary scholarship.

assessments.task_description:
Students will be provided with a selection of extracts drawn from texts studied in the first part of the course and asked to produce a 1000 word close reading.

The task requires a detailed analysis of literary language, form, style, and technique. Students should develop a clear argument about how meaning is created within the extract and support their interpretation through close engagement with the passage itself.

This assignment is designed to develop the skills of close reading and textual analysis that underpin advanced literary study. It also provides preparation for both the research essay and the final examination.

Guidelines, extracts, and marking criteria will be provided on Blackboard during the semester.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Please submit via TurnItIn on the link provided on Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Write a research essay of 2000 words in response to ONE of the questions provided.

Students will develop an original argument about one or more texts studied on the course, supported by close textual analysis and engagement with relevant scholarly criticism. Essays should demonstrate an understanding of literary form, historical and cultural context, and the critical debates relevant to the topic under discussion.

This assignment builds on the analytical skills developed in the Close Reading Assignment by requiring students to undertake independent research, engage with literary scholarship, and construct a sustained argument about Victorian and modern literature.

Guidelines, essay questions, and marking criteria will be provided on Blackboard during the semester.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Please submit via TurnItIn on the link provided on Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The final examination assesses students’ understanding of the major texts, writers, movements, and literary developments studied throughout the course. Students will be required to demonstrate knowledge of Victorian and modern literature and apply skills of literary analysis, comparison, and interpretation.

The examination may require students to engage with literary passages and to draw connections between texts, authors, and literary movements studied during the semester.

Further details regarding the format and structure of the examination will be provided during the semester.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1 Lecture: Introduction

We begin this module with an introduction to the course in general and to the Victorian Era in particular. I will outline some of the themes and ambivalences that we find in works of the Victorian era, many of which will emerge in the texts we’ll be reading in the following weeks. The scientific, technological, and socio-cultural of the period provide important context for the literature to come, and it is in many ways impossible to think through that literature without some kind of grasp of the milieu in which they were written, read, received, and published.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

The scientific, technological, and socio-cultural of the period provide important context for the literature to come, and it is in many ways impossible to think through that literature without some kind of grasp of the milieu in which they were written, read, received, and published.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2 Lecture: Victorian Poetry

This week is dedicated to Victorian poetry, a form deeply interested in this dichotomy between poetry and eloquence, which speaks to a distinction in attitudes and understandings of the purpose of art, specifically poetry, as either an act of meditation, and retreat into solitude, turning the mind inward upon its own thoughts and emotions, or a means of getting out into society, addressing audiences, rubbing shoulders with people, and participating in the world. Victorian poetry is deeply involved in that dichotomy, but it also committed to disturbing it in practice too, and in combining these different modes of writing in unstable mixtures. 


Copies of the set poems will be supplied in advance via Blackboard.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Victorian poetry is deeply involved in that dichotomy, but it also committed to disturbing it in practice too, and in combining these different modes of writing in unstable mixtures. Copies of the set poems will be supplied in advance via Blackboard.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4 Lecture: Tess of the D'Urbervilles

This week we turn to Tess of the D'Urbervilles, ,a novel written in the Realist tradition of the Victorian Era, and a novel that is deeply concerned with the social, cultural, sexual, religions traditions of that Era, and which sets out to show us, in no uncertain terms, the damage that can be done by those who hold steadfastly to social conventions and rules. Thomas Hardy was a man highly critical of his Late Victorian society, who used his works of literature to expose and explore its hypocrisies, its cruelties, and its tragedies, but who did so very much within the forms and narrative structures of that era. 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5: Lecture: The Importance of Being Earnest

This week we move from the literary realism and naturalism of Thomas Hardy to the aestheticism of Oscar Wilde, focusing on The Importance of Being Earnest. After a brief outline of Wilde's life, we will move on to his social, cultural, and ideological position as an aesthete in late nineteenth-century Britain, before turning more fully to The Importance of Being Earnest as as our case study and an example of a well-made play. 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6 Lecture: Decadent Short Fiction

This week explores the decadent movement through short fiction that revels in artifice, sensation, and aesthetic transgression. We’ll consider how these texts challenge moral conventions, blur boundaries between beauty and decay, and stage the self as performance. Themes of desire, ennui, and excess will be central, as will the idea of art for art’s sake.

Copies of the set stories will be provided on blackboard.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7 Lecture: Heart of Darkness

This week we read Heart of Darkness, a novella by the Polish-English novelist Joseph Conrad, which recounts a sailor's story of his journey as steamer captain in the African interior. We will position this novel as a critique of European colonial rule in Africa, whilst also examining questions of power dynamics and morality. 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8 Lecture: The Wasteland

This week we turn to T.S. Eliot's 'The Wasteland' as a prime example of modernist poetry, focusing on formal experimentation, fragmentation, and the search for new modes of expression in the aftermath of cultural and historical upheaval.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9 Lecture: Howards End

This week, we move firmly into the twentieth century, with E.M. Forster's masterpiece, Howards End, first published in 1910. We will consider its treatment of social conventions, codes of conduct and romance in turn-of-the-century England.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

learning_activities.topic:
Week 11 Lecture: Modernist Short Fiction

This week we turn to modernist short stories by the New Zealand writer Katherine Mansfield, and Irish writer James Joyce. You are, of course, welcome to read more stories than the two set; however, we will be focusing our discussion on 'The Dead' and 'The Garden Party'. 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12 Lecture: Jacob's Room

Nearing the end of our course, we look at a modernist novel, Virginia Woolf's Jacob's Room, published in 1922. Woolf's third novel, Jacob's Room is her first truly experimental work.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

learning_activities.topic:
Week 13 Lecture: Lady Chatterley's Lover

Our final work on the course is D.H. Lawrence's Lady Chatterley's Lover, a work infamous for its explicit descriptions of sex, use of four-letter words, and depiction of a relationship between an upper-class woman and a working-class man. Perhaps most outrageous at the time, though, was the author's portrayal of female sexual pleasure.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
